coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of N, P Co-doped orange carbon quantum dots with novel optical properties for bio-imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Naixin
Lei, Fang
Xu, Dandan
Li, Yong
Liu, Jinliang
Shi, Ying
coredata.dc:description: Chemical doping with heteroatoms can modulate physicochemical and photochemical properties of carbon dots (CDs). However, the development of multi heteroatoms co-doped CDs is still in early stage. In this work, a facile solvothermal synthesis strategy was applied to prepare multi heteroatoms (nitrogen and phosphorus) codoped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) using formamide and phosphoric acid as nitrogen source and phosphorus source, respectively. Compared with other CDs, the multi heteroatoms lead to dramatic improvement in the electronic characteristics and surface states. Thus, as the change of excitation wavelength, exciton recombination at different energy levels can be achieved, exhibiting the tunable luminescence from blue to orange regions. The prepared carbon dots have excitation dependence in the blue region, excitation independence in the orange region and high fluorescence stability. Its’ quantum yield (QY) is up to 47%. Plus, N, P-CDs is non-toxic to cells and can be used in cell imaging, which indicates carbon dots can be a potentially excellent bioimaging probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro imaging and UV light irradiation assay
PL intensity of CDs under different reaction conditions
Cytotoxicity assay and fluorescence imaging of Hela cells in vitro
Application of N, P CDs in Cell imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 21301115 ] and the Open Fund of Key Laboratory of Advanced Display and System Applications of Ministry of Education (Shanghai University) 201902 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of CDs, (b) size distribution histogram, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) Raman spectrum of the CDs under 180 °C, 6 h, 1 g citric acid and 0.25 g phosphorus acid and 15 mL formamide.

(a) XPS spectrum, magnification and fitting curves of (b) C 1s peak, (c) O 1s peak, (d) N 1s peak and (e) P 2p peak for the as prepared CDs. Magnification and fitting curves of (b) C 1s peak, (c) O 1s peak, (d) N 1s peak and (e) P 2p peak for the as prepared CDs.

PL spectra (λex = 540 nm, λem = 579 nm) of N, P- CDs synthesized at different condition (a) reaction temperature (120–200 °C), (b) reaction time (2–10 h) (c) various citric acid content (0.3–2 g), (d) phosphoric acid content (0–0.5 g).

(a) UV–vis absorption of the N, P-CDs (b) PL emission spectra under diﬀerent excitation wavelengths in the range of 330–600 nm (excitation wavelength interval 30 nm) of the N, P-CDs (the inset shows a photograph of the N, P-CDs solution under an excitation wavelength of 570 nm).
Effects of (a) irradiation time, (b) various concentrations of NaCl in aqueous solution on PL intensity of N, P-CDs (λex = 540 nm).
Cell toxicity of CDs on HeLa cells viability.

Optical images of HeLa cells incubated in the absence of CDs under (a) bright field and (b) 405 nm light. Cell imaging of CDs: (c) bright field microphotograph (d) confocal fluorescence microphotograph (e) overlap of the corresponding bright field image and fluorescence image of HeLa cells incubated with the CDs (λex = 405 nm, λem = 460 nm).
Schematic illustration of the one-step solvothermal synthesis of N, P-CDs.
Schematic illustration of proposed energy level and electron transition diagrams of N, P-CDs.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of N, P Co-doped orange carbon quantum dots with novel optical properties for bio-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, 99 Shang Da Road, Shanghai, 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, 99 Shang Da Road, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemical doping with heteroatoms can modulate physicochemical and photochemical properties of carbon dots (CDs). However, the development of multi heteroatoms co-doped CDs is still in early stage. In this work, a facile solvothermal synthesis strategy was applied to prepare multi heteroatoms (nitrogen and phosphorus) codoped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) using formamide and phosphoric acid as nitrogen source and phosphorus source, respectively. Compared with other CDs, the multi heteroatoms lead to dramatic improvement in the electronic characteristics and surface states. Thus, as the change of excitation wavelength, exciton recombination at different energy levels can be achieved, exhibiting the tunable luminescence from blue to orange regions. The prepared carbon dots have excitation dependence in the blue region, excitation independence in the orange region and high fluorescence stability. Its’ quantum yield (QY) is up to 47%. Plus, N, P-CDs is non-toxic to cells and can be used in cell imaging, which indicates carbon dots can be a potentially excellent bioimaging probe.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, nanotechnology is widely used in biomedical fields, especially bioimaging in vivo and in vitro. Among many nanomaterials, fluorescent quantum dots (QDs) have superior optical properties [1,2]. Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots, such as CdSe, ZnS, carbon dots have lower toxicity and higher biocompatibility, they have more advantages in the field of biological imaging and so on [3–6].

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have become a promising luminescent material, which attract great attention in various fields such as biosensing [7–10], ion detective [11], pH probe [12], and optoelectronics [13] for their good stability, low toxicity, excellent resistance to photobleaching and good biocompatibility [14,15]. CDs are a new kind of nano-sized carbon materials with size within 10 nm, which were firstly found by Xu in 2004 by purification of the carbon nanomaterials family [16]. However, the low fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and self-quenching of solid-state CDs still hinder further development [17], and that can be ascribed to excessive surface detects induced by dangling bonds or the direct π–π interactions and the aggregation of nanoparticles [18]. Moreover, CDs usually have a strong blue emission under UV excitation [19,20], on account of the low penetration depth of excitation light and the presence of bioluminescence interference, further biological applications are prevented [21,22].

Heteroatom doping is a highly efficient and convenient approach to tune the optical properties and passivate the surface of CDs. Heteroatom doped CDs have attracted great attention and develop fast in recent years for simple preparation process and novel optical performance. For example, Zhuo synthesized sulphur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots via one step hydrothermal treatment of ionic liquid and PEG [23]. Guo fabricated fluorescent nitrogen–doped carbon dots by a facile one–pot solid phase pyrolysis process [24]. The doping atoms in the carbon structure introduces localized states which can trap photo-excited electrons and endow new PL properties such as red shift of luminescence [25]. To data, the main heteroatoms are nitrogen, phosphorus, sulphur, boron and fluorine. According to references, all these elements doping can improve the optical performance of CDs [6,9,26–28].

Nitrogen and phosphorus are elements of the same family in the periodic table, they have similar outer shell electronic structure, same number of valence electrons [29]. Nitrogen is a general doping atom in the preparation of CDs, it has a radius close to that of the carbon, and because it has five valence electrons in its outermost shell, it can bond tightly to the carbon atoms. Due to the doping of N, the CDs become n-type donor [30]. While phosphorus is doped in CDs, when it acts as n-type donor and cause a substitutional defect in sp3-bonded diamond thin ﬁlms [31]. Thus, the electronic characteristics of CDs can be tuned by doping N and P in order to create more surface defect as active sites, which generate new energy levels and induce unanticipated PL properties. More recently, nitrogen and phosphorus have been reported used as heteroatoms to prepare the doped CDs [32]. What's more, CDs are easily agglomerated, N and P doping can passivate surface of CDs, new functional groups containing N and P can attach on the surface of CDs, which will reduce nonradiative transition, improving the QY and prevent the aggregation of CDs due to high steric hindrance.

Herein, we prepared N, P-CDs by one-step solvothermal method. P doping introduces new surface states lower than C state and N state, which can narrow the band gap, causing redshift of the excitation and emission wavelength [26,33]. Doping with N and P extends the absorption of CDs from UV region to visible region (561 nm). Its luminescence has excitation dependence in blue region and luminescence independence in orange region, which is a novel property of CDs. The N, P-CDs show a relatively high PLQY ~47% in the orange emission region. In addition, we applied the N, P-CDs to cell imaging and cell culture. The results indicate the N, P-CDs can be clearly imaged in cytoplasm and are non-toxic to cells.

Citric acid (CA), phosphoric acid, formamide were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

As shown in Scheme 1 , we chose citric acid as carbon source, and phosphoric acid, formamide as dopant, respectively. The N, P-CDs were synthesized by one step solvothermal method at 180 °C for 6 h. The experimental steps are as follows:

Dissolved 0.3–2 g citric acid into 15 mL formamide, with stirring until thoroughly mixed. Then added 0–0.5 g phosphoric acid into the solution, stirred until the solution fully mixed. The obtained transparent solution was transferred into 20 mL Teflon-sealed autoclave at 120–200 °C for 2–10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained brown-red solution was centrifuged once at 11,000 rpm for 10 min, then with 0.22 μm microporous membrane filtration to remove small particle impurities. After dialysis for 48 h and lyophilization, the CDs powder was obtained. Aqueous solution of 3 mg/mL was also prepared for later use.

Human cervical carcinoma cells (HeLa cells) were selected and cultured in an incubator with Dulbecco’ s Modified Eagle’ s Medium (DMEM) containing 10% (vol−1) fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin (100 μg mL−1) and streptomycin (100 μg mL−1) at 37 °C with 5% CO2. To investigate in vitro cytotoxicity of the as-prepared CDs, Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assays were examined. Briefly, cells were seeded onto a 96-well plate at a density of 8 × 103 per well cultured overnight (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then, the culture medium was replaced by fresh culture medium containing nanomaterials at different concentrations (0, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350 μg mL−1) for 24 h and 48 h. After being rinsed with PBS twice, each group was added into 100 μL fresh DMEM containing 10 μL of CCK-8 Kit. After incubating for an additional 2 h, the absorbance at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader (SPARK, TECAN). The cell viability (%) was calculated from following equation: (Absorbance treated group/Absorbance the control group) × 100%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro imaging and UV light irradiation assay

ce:section.ce:para: Cells were seeded onto well cultured overnight, new culture medium containing 0.2 mg/mL N, P-CDs added in well and incubated for 2 h. Then washed by PBS and imaged by using a confocal laser microscope (Olympus FV 3000, Japan).

Cells were seeded onto a 96-well plate at a density of 8 × 103 per well cultured overnight. Medium were replaced by new CDs-medium mixture with a concentration of 0, 300 μg/mL and incubated for 24 h. After incubation for 24 h, one of the wells was taken out and placed at an external light source for 0.5, 1 and 2 min at a wavelength of 365 nm (3 W, 408 mW/cm2) or 405 nm (3 W, 832 mW/cm2). Then put back in the incubator for 2 h detect the absorbency.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained by using an RF5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a Hitachi-7650 transmission electron microscope at 200 kV (Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) analyses were performed on a Wscalab X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, UK). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on a Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). Raman spectra were recorded using INVIA Microscopic Confocal Raman Spectrometer (Renishaw PLC, England) under He–Ne laser with an excitation wavelength of 632.8 nm. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were performed on a UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The QY of the as-prepared CDs was measured by the slope method.

A solution of fluorescein in 0.1 mol/L NaOH (QY of 79% at 360 nm, η = 1.33) was used as the standard. The value of the quantum yield was calculated according to the following equation:
Q x
=
Q
s t
(
K x
/
K
s t
)
(
n x
/
n
s t
)
2
where Q is QY, K is the slope, η is the refractive index with 1.33 as the default for both cresyl violet and CDs. The subscript ‘st’ refers to the cresyl violet, while the subscript ‘x’ refers to the samples.

TEM measurements were carried out to observe the morphology of N, P-CDs. As can be seen from Fig. 1 (a), the as-prepared CDs have spherical structure, uniformly distributed and no obvious aggregation due to lots of ligands bonded on the surface of CDs. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CDs show well-resolved lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.24 nm, which correspond to the (100) diﬀraction facets of graphite. It proves that the as-prepared CDs have carbon core that is similar to the graphite structure, which is mostly composed of sp2 hybridization. From Fig. 1 (b), the diameter of as-prepared CDs is around 2.75–4.25 nm, with the average size of 3.35 nm.

XRD result confirms the nano structure of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 (c), there is a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.6°, the interlayer spacing is 0.37 nm, larger than the interlayer spacing (0.34) of (002) in graphite (2θ = 26.6°). It was speculated that the reason that TEM and XRD results are inconsistent is these two characterization methods detect different lattice planes of N, P-CDs. Although the radius of N atoms is smaller than C atoms, the radius of the P atoms is larger than C atoms, so P doping enlarges the spacing. Therefore, the increase in lattice spacing can indirectly indicates that P enters into the crystalized sp2 C core of the CDs and induce defects in CDs [34].

Raman spectroscopy examines the degree of graphitization of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 (d), the characteristic peaks at 1331 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1 correspond to D band and G band, respectively. D band represents the degree of disorder and lattice defects in graphene structure; G band represents the highly ordered graphene. From the Raman spectroscopy, the ratio of the intensity of D band and G band (ID/IG = 1.06) is slightly higher than that of the original graphite film (ID/IG~1.05) [34]. This indicated that the CDs synthesized by our one-step method have lower crystallinity due to the doping of N and P. In comparison with graphene [9], the CDs exhibit a broader D band, also suggesting that the doping of N and P atoms into the conjugated carbon backbone can lead to slight disordered structure.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum was performed to investigate functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared N, P-CDs (Fig. 2 ). The wide absorption bands at 3160 cm−1 and 3419 cm−1 are derived from the vibration of O–H and N–H groups [35]. The characteristic peak at 2855 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C–H. The peaks at 1569 cm−1 and 1447 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of CO and bending vibration of C–N, respectively, which proves that the N has been introduced into CDs successfully. The other characteristic peaks are assigned to the ligands that containing N and O. The peaks at 960 cm−1, 1076 cm−1, and 1368 cm−1 are assigned to P–C, P–O–C (aromatic), and PO, respectively, which indicates that P has also been incorporated into the surface of CDs.

More information on diﬀerences of surface functional groups is further provided by XPS analysis. The high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 3(b)) reveals the presence of C–C (284.0 eV), CC (284.6 eV), C–N/C–P (285.7 eV), and O–CO (288 eV). From Fig. 3 (c), the O 1s spectra exhibit two peaks at 531.5 and 533.3 eV, which attributed to CO and C–O groups, respectively. From Fig. 3 (d), high-resolution N 1s spectra reveal that N exists in the form of pyridinic N of = N – (398.4 ev) and the pyrrolic N of – NH – (399.7 ev), respectively. As previously reported, the pyrrolic N exposes the edge of the plane and the defect parts, and the pyridinic N replaces C atoms at the edge or plane of the defect parts [36]. Thus, XPS confirms that N not only participated in the formation of surface functional ligands, but also incorporated the crystalized core of CDs, which is consistent with Raman results as this paper mentioned above. In the high-resolution P 2p spectra (Fig. 3 (e)), 133.1 ev and 134.5 ev absorption peaks are assigned to P–N/P–C and P–O groups. These results from XPS data are in good accordance with FTIR analysis. O 1s and P 2p high-resolution spectra confirm P can bond on the surface of CDs, which is consistent with the FTIR results. All results of XPS and FTIR spectra clearly conﬁrmed that N and P atoms had doped into the N, P-CDS. It was deduced that the electronic properties and surface states of N, P-CDs can be improved due to the introduction of N and P atoms. The new surface ligands containing N and P doping can significantly induce the surface state and create higher rate of radioactive recombination [33,37].
Meanwhile, all the analysis suggests that there are carbonyl groups, hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. All of them are hydrophilic groups, increase the solubility of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL intensity of CDs under different reaction conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows PL spectra of N, P-CDs prepared by different conditions under 540 nm excitation. In order to obtain the maximum PL intensity, the reaction parameters: temperature, reaction time, quantity of citric acid and phosphorus acid have been investigated. As showed in Fig. 4 (a), the emission spectra of different temperature (from 120 °C to 200 °C) shows two peaks located at 445 nm (blue region) and 579 nm (orange region). As the reaction temperature increases, the emission wavelength at 579 nm remains constant, while the PL intensity increases gradually, and reached the maximum at 180 °C. When the reaction temperature reached 200 °C, the PL intensity decreased. Similarly, from Fig. 4 (b), we fixed the reaction temperature at 180 °C and investigated the influence of reaction time. As the reaction time varies from 2 h to 10 h, the PL intensity at 579 nm gets significantly improved and then decreased, the optimal reaction time is 6 h. It shows that due to high temperature and prolonged reaction time, the structure of the N, P-CDs is damaged. As showed in Fig. 4 (c), as the quantity of citric acid increased from 0.3 g to 1 g, the PL intensity increased gradually and reaches maximum at the 1 g citric acid. However, when the concentration further increased, the PL intensity decreases, which indicated that the addition of excessive carbon source inhibits the formation of CDs [38]. Similarly, as for the quantity of phosphoric acid, the PL intensity gets maximum when the quantity of phosphoric acid is 0.25 g. In summary, the N, P-CDs with optimal PL intensity and narrowest FWHM in 579 nm can be prepared by solvothermal method at 180 °C for 6 h with 1 g citric acid and 0.25 g phosphorus acid and 15 mL formamide. The strongest orange fluorescence emission centered at 579 nm (λex = 540 nm) possesses a high quantum yield of 47%. We note that when phosphonic acid is not added, the emission peak at 579 nm decreased significantly, which further proves the importance of P doping. The red shift of PL compared to traditional blue emitting CDs can be ascribed to the addition of N in formamide and P in phosphorus acid. From Fig. 4 (d), after P doping, PL intensity gets improved obviously due to the PL performance of CDs correlated with the surface state and functional groups [33]. P provided by phosphorus acid can produce more various functional groups on surface [39]. In other words, this kind of heteroatom doping can give CDs more surface defect states, acting as more exciton radiative recombination center. The addition of N and P on surface can also passivate the surface of CDs, avoiding the aggregation caused by the high surface energy of nanoparticles.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum is shown in Fig. 5 (a). Three obvious absorption peaks are located at 273 nm, 363 nm and 561 nm, respectively. Normally, the absorption band at 273 nm is assigned to the π - π* conjugate transition of CC sp2 bond in the carbon core of CDs [40]. According to the reports, almost all carbon dots of different luminous colors show analogous 200–350 nm absorption in UV region, these peaks are originated from the conjugate sp2 structures in the core part of CDs [41]. The 363 nm and 561 nm at lower energy region originates from n - π* transition of CO, CN and P–N/P–C separately on the surface of N, P-CDs [37,42,43]. The three absorption peaks also correspond to our analysis of the structure of CDs. The excitation wavelength-dependent emission spectra of the N, P-CDs at the excitation wavelengths from 300 to 600 nm (excitation wavelength interval 30 nm) are shown in Fig. 5 b. At excitation wavelengths of under 330 nm, the emission peak located at 443 nm. With an increase of the excitation wavelengths (to 360–480 nm), only emission peak (from 443 to 570 nm) is observed. Further increasing the excitation wavelengths (510–570 nm) excites only one orange emission at 587 nm; while at even higher excitation wavelengths (600 nm), nearly no fluorescence emission is recorded. The strongest blue fluorescence emission centered at 453 nm (λex = 360 nm), while the strongest orange fluorescence emission band at 587 nm (λex = 570 nm), possesses a high quantum yield of 47%. These two emission bands at 443–460 nm and 587 nm correlate the absorption peaks at 363 nm and 561 nm, respectively, which show that emission at 443–460 nm is caused by N doping, emission at 587 is due to P doping.

Doping N and P can introduce new energy levels between the band gaps of CDs, where electrons and holes can recombine to fluorescent. In addition, the optical performance of CDs is mainly due to the existence of surface defects. Without surface passivation, surface dangling bonds tends to capture electrons by nonradiative way, while the surface modification can make excitons return to the ground state by radiative transition, resulting in fluorescence [44–46]. Co-doping a second element into N-doped carbon nanomaterials can affect the electronic properties, tune PL performance [39,40,47,48]. As shown in Scheme 2 , due to co-doping N and P, new highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO-N and HOMO-P) can be generated. According to Kang et al. [26], λem of heteroatoms doped CDs is related to the electronegativity (χ) of doped atoms. N-doping is responsible for the blue fluorescence region of CDs (443 nm–460 nm). The luminescence dependence in the blue region (440 nm–460 nm) mainly comes from advantages of N, such as radius close to C, higher electronegativity than C, five valence bonds to be connected, and a pair of lone pair electrons with high affinity [49]. The electronegativity of N, C, P is 3.04, 2.5, and 2.1, respectively. Because P has lower electronegativity than N, can be an electron donor, new P states are formed between LUMO (π*) and HOMO (π), which can enable N, P-CDs absorb light with longer wavelength and red shift of emission wavelength. Thus, the incorporation of N and P can introduce new energy levels between the LUMO and HOMO.

The π-π* transition (black) comes from the absorption peak at 273 nm, which is from conjugated sp2 region in the graphite-like core [42]. The n (N)-π* transition (blue) originates from the absorption peak of CO, CN at 363 nm [5]. The n (P)-π* transition (red) from the 561 nm absorption peak is due to P–N/P–C [37,48,50]. When HOMO-2 (π) excitons transit to LUMO (ν = 0, λabs = 273 nm), π*-n (p) or π*-n (N) recombination rarely occurs, and the energy of exciton recombination released as nonradiative recombination (black). When the absorption wavelength is 363 nm, the electrons at HOMO-1 (n (N)) transit to UMO, after relaxation, they reach LUMO (v = 0), and then transit back to the ground state (HOMO-1 n (N)), the energy is released in the form of blue light. However, the position of N in N, P-CDs is not fixed, the orbital energy changes, which results in more HOMO-1 energy levels (n'(N)). Therefore, as the excitation wavelength becomes longer, the blue fluorescence by excitons recombination will red shift (443–570 nm). Therefore, the emission of N, P-CDs in the blue region (443–570 nm) is excitation-dependent. However, since the doping amount of P is lower than N, the bigger difference between the atomic radii of P and N, and the electronegativity (χ) is lower than C, as shown in Scheme 2, the HOMO (n (p)) generated by P–N/P–C is relatively single. Therefore, as the excitation wavelength continues to redshift, the position of the emission wavelength remains unchanged at 587 nm, and as the redshift of the excitation wavelength causes more electrons on HOMO (n (p)) to be excited, the intensity of 587 nm light was increased, and the intensity reached the highest when the excitation wavelength was 570 nm. As a result, N, P-CDs have excitation independence in the orange region.

The fluorescent stability of CDs is a key factor in its application in various fields. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), after irradiation under an UV lamp (365 nm) from 0 to 60 min, the PL intensity of the CDs aqueous solution doesn't change obviously. What's more, we characterized the PL intensity of CDs aqueous solution with NaCl concentration from 0.00 to 2.00 mol/L to investigate the effect of salinity on the PL of the CDs. Fig. 6 (b) shows the salinity has no effect on PL intensity of N, P-CDs. The results show N, P-CDs can resist the light bleaching and high salinity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay and fluorescence imaging of Hela cells in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: An in vitro CCK-8 assay was used to test the toxicity of N, P-CDs. As shown in Fig. 7 , the N, P-CDs cause less than 5% decrease in cell viability at a concentration of 350 μg/mL after exposing 24 h. What's more, the viability remains more than 95% after 48 h exposure. These results indicate that N, P-CDs can potentially be used in bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N, P CDs in Cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the application of N, P-CDs in cellular imaging, we performed in vitro cellular studies using HeLa cells. Fig. 8 (a) and (b) shows the images of HeLa cells under bright field and 405 nm light with no CDs. Without CDs solution, no obvious fluorescence emission was observed in bright field and blank HeLa cells under excitation of 405 nm. This phenomenon indicates that HeLa cells themselves do not emit fluorescence and have no effect on fluorescence properties in following experiments.

As shown in Fig. 8 (c), HeLa cells itself did not fluorescent in the bright field. After incubation in 0.2 mg/mL CDs solution for 2 h, as shown in Fig. 8 (d), the cells display blue fluorescence (460 nm) under 405 nm excitation. From the bright field (Fig. 8 (c)) and confocal overlap photographs (Fig. 8 (e)), most N, P-CDs were observed in the cell cytoplasm, indicating that the N, P-CDs particles had been internalized by the cells. And N, P-CDs have no damage impact on morphology of cells, indicating that the HeLa cells are still alive. However, because the emission wavelength close to that of excitation light, the ideal image of orange-dyed cells cannot be obtained. It is necessary to further redshift the emission peak by modifying the CDs to improve its practicability in the application of bio-imaging. In addition, the intensity of fluorescence signal doesn't attenuate significantly after exposure to ultraviolet lamp for a long time. The experimental results show that the N, P-CDs have excellent biocompatibility and fluorescence stability, and are expected to become a new generation of biofluorescence probe materials. In consideration of bioimaging with CDs under UV–Vis light, we investigated cell toxicity during 365 and 405 nm light irradiation with/without CDs. As shown in Figure S1, cell viability decreased gradually with exposure time increased, caused by high-energy level of UV light. Nevertheless, cell viability was higher when irradiated with 405 nm compared to that irradiated at 365 nm without CDs, as well as incubated with CDs where cell viability was 61.7% irradiated for 2 min at 405 nm. As a matter of fact, exposure time under UV light for bioimaging is less than 30 s, which causes hardly damage to normal cell or tissue.

In summary, the metal-free, highly fluorescent and low toxicity N, P-CDs were synthesized by a simple solvothermal method with citric acid, formamide and phosphoric acid as starting materials. The carbon dots prepared by co-doping N and P have excitation dependence in the blue region (443–460 nm) and excitation independence in the orange region (587 nm). The fluorescence stability is desirable, and the quantum yield (QY) reaches 47%. Through TEM, N, P-CDs are spherical-like structures with uniform dispersion and have an average particle diameter of 3.35 nm. It is further confirmed by FT-IR and XPS tests that N and P are successfully incorporated into the structure of CDs. The successful application of CDs to cell imaging and UV light irradiation experiments demonstrate that the N, P-CDs are potential alternatives for the conventional expensive dyes due to their stable fluorescence, low toxicity and excellent biocompatible.

Naixin Li: Investigation, Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation. Fang Lei: Conceptualization, Visualization, Validation, Writing - Review & Editing. Dandan Xu: Investigation, Data curation. Yong Li: Methodology, Software, Investigation. Jinliang Liu: Resources, Visualization, Writing - Review & Editing. Ying Shi: Supervision, Project administration and Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 21301115] and the Open Fund of Key Laboratory of Advanced Display and System Applications of Ministry of Education (Shanghai University) 201902.
